title: Scoping Server Permissions
concept: Restricting what a server can actually do (read-only file access, sandboxed execution) at the server level.

exercises.prompt: In our last lesson, we discussed 'Human-in-the-Loop' tool approval. What does this practice require?

options.text: Letting the LLM automatically run any command it wants.
Requiring explicit human approval before a sensitive tool action is executed.
Writing code that automatically fixes bugs without user notification.

exercises.explain: Human-in-the-Loop (HITL) approval requires a human user to review and manually confirm sensitive actions before they are executed.

cards.title: Defense in Depth
cards.body: We know humans should approve risky actions, but what if a server gets compromised and tries to read your private keys without even asking? We need a second line of defense: locking down the server's actual system privileges.

cards.title: Scoping Server Permissions
cards.body: Scoping server permissions means restricting what an MCP server can access on your host system. By enforcing rules like read-only paths and sandboxed environments, you ensure the server cannot delete files or run harmful system commands.

cards.title: The Library Guest Pass
cards.body: Think of a scoped server like a library guest pass. Instead of giving a visitor the keys to the entire building, you give them a pass that only lets them read books in the history room, preventing them from entering the staff offices.

cards.title: Locked in the Workspace
cards.body: An MCP file-manager server is configured with access restricted only to '/workspace/projects'. If an LLM asks the server to read '/etc/passwd' or delete a system file, the server returns an access denied error.

cards.title: LLMs Aren't Security Guards
cards.body: You might think that because the LLM is instructed to be good, the server tools are safe. In reality, LLMs can be tricked by prompt injection, making hard-coded server-side permission boundaries absolutely essential.

cards.title: Unrestricted vs. Scoped
cards.body: Restricting server access at the system level provides hard boundaries that prompt injections cannot break.

left.text: Full disk read/write access. A malicious prompt could delete your entire operating system.

right.text: Read-only access restricted to one folder. Safe from system-wide destructive actions.

exercises.prompt: What does configuring an MCP server with 'read-only' file access achieve?

options.text: It allows the server to write new system files.
It allows the server to view files but prevents any modifications or deletions.
It completely blocks the server from seeing any files.
It forces the user to type a password for every file read.

exercises.explain: Read-only access ensures the server can view files to assist the user, but cannot modify, overwrite, or delete any data on the disk.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Scoping server permissions at the system level acts as a backup safety net if a human mistakenly approves a dangerous tool action.
exercises.explain: Even if a user accidentally approves a harmful delete tool call, a properly scoped server restricted to read-only access will block the deletion at the system level.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the security rule:
exercises.sentence: To protect your main operating system, always run untrusted MCP servers inside an isolated sandbox.
exercises.explain: Running an MCP server inside an isolated container (a sandbox) ensures it cannot access your main computer's system resources or sensitive files.

exercises.prompt: Identify which of these tool execution requests represents a malicious attempt to bypass scoped directory access:

options.text: 'Reading system files at ../../../etc/shadow to extract credentials.'
'Listing the files inside the current project folder.'

exercises.explain: Option B shows an AI agent attempting a classic directory traversal attack to read sensitive system files, which scoping permissions is designed to block.

exercises.prompt: Why is restricting a filesystem server to 'read-only' and running it in a sandbox considered a robust defense-in-depth strategy for LLM applications?

options.text: It forces the user to manually approve every single read request.
It prevents malicious commands from damaging the host system even if the LLM is compromised.
It encrypts all data sent between the client and the server.
It speeds up the server's execution time by skipping security checks.

exercises.explain: Limiting a server's environment to read-only access and sandboxing its execution ensures that even if the LLM is tricked into generating a malicious command, the server physically lacks the operating system permissions to execute it.
